quiz.title: How Client-Server Works
quiz.subtitle: Requests, responses, and the architecture behind the web
quiz.description: Test your understanding of client-server architecture — from basic request flows to load balancing, scaling, and proxy patterns.

questions.text: In client-server architecture, which side initiates a request?

options.text: The server
The client
Both sides simultaneously
A third-party broker always initiates the exchange

questions.explanation: In the client-server model, the client always initiates communication by sending a request. The server listens passively and responds to incoming requests.

wrongAnswerFeedback.A: Servers listen and respond; they do not initiate requests in the classic client-server model.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: Traditional client-server is asymmetric — only the client initiates. Technologies like WebSockets enable bidirectional communication but that is a different pattern.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: A broker is used in message queue architectures, not the classic client-server pattern.

questions.text: What is the role of a server in client-server architecture?

options.text: It browses the internet on behalf of users
It provides resources or services to clients that request them
It stores only static files and cannot run any logic
It connects directly to end-user hardware to run applications

questions.explanation: A server is a program or machine that provides resources (files, data, computation) or services to clients that request them. Servers listen on a network port and respond to client requests.

wrongAnswerFeedback.A: Browsing is what a client (browser) does, not a server.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: Servers can run application logic, process data, query databases, and much more — they are not limited to static files.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: Servers communicate over a network; they do not connect directly to end-user hardware to run apps.

questions.text: What does DNS stand for and what is its primary purpose?

options.text: Data Network Service — routes packets between servers
Domain Name System — translates domain names to IP addresses
Distributed Node Service — balances load across servers
Dynamic Network Security — encrypts domain lookups

questions.explanation: DNS (Domain Name System) acts as the internet's phone book. It translates human-readable domain names (e.g., example.com) into the IP addresses that computers use to identify each other on a network.

wrongAnswerFeedback.A: Packet routing is handled by routers using protocols like BGP, not DNS.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: Load balancing is a separate concern handled by load balancers or DNS-level round-robin.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: DNS over HTTPS (DoH) and DNSSEC exist for encryption, but DNS itself is the name resolution system.

questions.text: What is the main advantage of separating client and server responsibilities in the client-server model?

options.text: It allows independent development and scaling of each side
It requires fewer network protocols
It eliminates the need for authentication
It guarantees zero downtime

questions.explanation: Separating client and server allows each to be developed, deployed, and scaled independently. This modularity is a key reason for the success of the client-server model.

wrongAnswerFeedback.B: The number of protocols is not affected by the separation of concerns.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: Authentication is still required in most systems.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: Downtime can still occur due to failures on either side.

questions.text: What is an IP address used for?

options.text: Encrypting data sent between client and server
Uniquely identifying a device on a network so data can be routed to it
Storing the domain names of websites
Authenticating users before they can connect to a server

questions.explanation: An IP (Internet Protocol) address is a numerical label assigned to each device on a network. It serves two main functions: host or network interface identification, and location addressing — allowing packets to be routed to the correct destination.

wrongAnswerFeedback.A: Encryption is handled by TLS/SSL, not IP addresses.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: Domain names are stored and resolved by DNS, not by IP addresses.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: Authentication is handled by protocols like OAuth, JWT, or passwords — not by IP addresses.

questions.text: Why is statelessness considered an advantage in web server design?

options.text: It allows servers to handle requests without tracking user sessions, making scaling easier
It increases the security of the network layer
It guarantees faster response times for all requests
It eliminates the need for databases

questions.explanation: Statelessness means each request contains all the information needed for processing, so servers do not need to remember previous interactions. This makes it much easier to add or remove servers to handle varying loads (horizontal scaling).

wrongAnswerFeedback.B: Statelessness is not a direct security feature.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: Statelessness does not guarantee faster responses, but it does improve scalability.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: Databases are still needed for persistent data.

questions.text: What is a common drawback of tightly coupling client and server logic?

options.text: It makes updates and scaling more difficult
It improves modularity and maintainability
It allows for more flexible network protocols
It reduces the need for testing

questions.explanation: When client and server logic are tightly coupled, changes to one often require changes to the other, making updates, maintenance, and scaling more difficult.

wrongAnswerFeedback.B: Tight coupling reduces modularity and maintainability.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: Protocol flexibility is not improved by tight coupling.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: Testing is still required and may be more complex.

questions.text: What is the primary purpose of a load balancer in a client-server system?

options.text: To compress responses and reduce bandwidth usage
To distribute incoming client requests across multiple server instances
To cache static assets closer to the end user
To authenticate users before requests reach the application servers

questions.explanation: A load balancer sits in front of a pool of servers and distributes incoming requests among them using algorithms such as round-robin, least connections, or IP hash. This improves availability, fault tolerance, and throughput — if one server fails, the load balancer routes traffic to healthy instances.

wrongAnswerFeedback.A: Compression is typically configured in the web server or application (e.g., gzip), not the load balancer's primary role.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: Caching assets closer to users is the role of a CDN (Content Delivery Network), not a load balancer.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: Authentication is an application-layer concern (identity provider, API gateway); load balancers operate at layer 4 or 7 and are not authentication systems.

questions.text: What is the difference between horizontal scaling and vertical scaling?

options.text: Horizontal scaling adds more CPU/RAM to one machine; vertical scaling adds more machines
Horizontal scaling adds more machines to a pool; vertical scaling upgrades hardware on existing machines
Horizontal scaling is only possible with NoSQL databases; vertical scaling is for relational databases
Both terms describe the same concept but apply to different cloud providers

questions.explanation: Horizontal scaling (scale out) means adding more server instances to distribute load. Vertical scaling (scale up) means adding more resources (CPU, RAM, storage) to an existing machine. Horizontal scaling is generally preferred for stateless web applications because it offers near-unlimited capacity and better fault tolerance.

wrongAnswerFeedback.A: This definition is reversed — adding hardware to one machine is vertical (scale up), adding machines is horizontal (scale out).
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: Both scaling strategies apply to any type of server or database regardless of SQL vs. NoSQL.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: These are standard industry terms with distinct meanings across all cloud providers and infrastructure types.

questions.text: What is a reverse proxy and how does it differ from a forward proxy?

options.text: A forward proxy sits in front of servers and shields them from clients; a reverse proxy sits in front of clients and shields them from servers
A reverse proxy sits in front of servers and handles requests on their behalf; a forward proxy sits in front of clients and handles requests on their behalf
They are the same architectural pattern with different names in different regions
A reverse proxy only works with HTTPS; a forward proxy only works with HTTP

questions.explanation: A forward proxy acts on behalf of clients — it intercepts outbound requests and can provide anonymity, access control, or caching for the client side. A reverse proxy acts on behalf of servers — it intercepts inbound requests, hiding server details and enabling load balancing, SSL termination, compression, and caching. Nginx and HAProxy are common reverse proxies.

wrongAnswerFeedback.A: This swaps the positions — a reverse proxy is in front of servers (not clients), and a forward proxy is in front of clients (not servers).
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: They are fundamentally different in placement and purpose, not regional naming variants.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: Both proxy types can work with HTTP and HTTPS; the protocol does not determine the proxy type.

questions.text: What is a thin client in client-server architecture?

options.text: A client that relies on the server for most processing and logic
A client that performs all processing locally
A client that only stores static files
A client that acts as a server for other clients

questions.explanation: A thin client is designed to be lightweight, offloading most processing and logic to the server. Web browsers are a common example.

wrongAnswerFeedback.B: A fat (thick) client performs most processing locally.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: Static file storage is not the defining feature of a thin client.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: Clients do not act as servers in the classic client-server model.

questions.text: How does a RESTful API embody the principles of client-server architecture?

options.text: It separates user interface concerns from data storage and logic
It requires all clients to be web browsers
It eliminates the need for HTTP
It forces all logic into the client

questions.explanation: RESTful APIs are designed around the separation of concerns: the client handles presentation, while the server manages data and logic. This separation is a core principle of client-server architecture.

wrongAnswerFeedback.B: RESTful APIs can be consumed by any client, not just browsers.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: RESTful APIs typically use HTTP.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: Logic is not forced into the client; the server still handles core logic.

questions.text: What is the main role of an API gateway in a client-server system?

options.text: To provide a single entry point for client requests and route them to appropriate backend services
To store all application data
To replace the need for load balancers
To act as a DNS resolver

questions.explanation: An API gateway acts as a single entry point for clients, handling routing, authentication, rate limiting, and aggregating responses from multiple backend services.

wrongAnswerFeedback.B: Data storage is handled by databases, not API gateways.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: API gateways and load balancers serve different roles.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: DNS resolution is not the role of an API gateway.

questions.text: Which of the following best describes a fat (thick) client?

options.text: A client that offloads most processing to the server
A client that performs significant processing locally and may work offline
A client that only displays static HTML
A client that acts as a proxy server

questions.explanation: A fat (thick) client performs much of the processing locally, sometimes even when offline. Examples include desktop applications and some mobile apps.

wrongAnswerFeedback.C: Static HTML display is not the defining feature of a fat client.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: Clients do not act as proxy servers in this context.

questions.text: Why are APIs important in client-server architecture?

options.text: They define clear contracts for communication between client and server
They eliminate the need for servers
They guarantee zero downtime
They force all logic into the client

questions.explanation: APIs (Application Programming Interfaces) define how clients and servers interact, specifying endpoints, data formats, and expected behaviors. This contract enables interoperability and modularity.

wrongAnswerFeedback.B: Servers are still required to provide resources and logic.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: APIs do not guarantee uptime.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: Logic can be on either side, depending on the API design.

questions.text: What is the main benefit of using a service-oriented architecture (SOA) in client-server systems?

options.text: It enables independent deployment and scaling of services
It requires all services to be written in the same language
It eliminates the need for clients
It guarantees no network failures

questions.explanation: SOA allows different services to be developed, deployed, and scaled independently, improving flexibility and maintainability.

wrongAnswerFeedback.B: Services in SOA can be written in any language as long as they communicate via agreed protocols.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: Clients are still needed to consume services.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: Network failures can still occur in any architecture.

questions.text: How does a CDN (Content Delivery Network) complement client-server architecture?

options.text: By caching and serving content closer to clients, reducing latency
By replacing the need for servers
By storing only DNS records
By forcing all traffic through a single server

questions.explanation: A CDN caches content at edge locations near users, reducing latency and offloading traffic from origin servers.

wrongAnswerFeedback.B: CDNs complement, not replace, origin servers.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: CDNs cache and serve content, not just DNS records.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: CDNs distribute traffic, not centralize it.

questions.text: What is the main security risk if a client blindly trusts all data from a server?

options.text: It may be vulnerable to malicious data or code, leading to security breaches
It will always run faster
It will never need to validate input
It guarantees data integrity

questions.explanation: Clients must validate and sanitize all data received from servers to prevent attacks such as XSS, CSRF, or injection attacks.

wrongAnswerFeedback.B: Security is not related to speed.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: Input validation is always necessary.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: Trusting all data does not guarantee integrity.

questions.text: How does session management typically work in stateless client-server systems?

options.text: Session state is stored on the client and passed with each request (e.g., cookies, tokens)
Session state is always stored on the server only
Sessions are not possible in stateless systems
Session management is handled by DNS

questions.explanation: In stateless systems, session data is typically stored on the client (e.g., in cookies or tokens) and sent with each request, allowing the server to remain stateless.

wrongAnswerFeedback.B: Storing all session state on the server makes the system stateful.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: Sessions are possible using client-side storage.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: DNS does not manage sessions.

questions.text: What is the main reason for using microservices in modern client-server systems?

options.text: To break down applications into smaller, independently deployable services
To force all logic into the client
To eliminate the need for APIs
To guarantee zero downtime

questions.explanation: Microservices architecture decomposes applications into small, independent services that can be developed, deployed, and scaled separately, increasing agility and resilience.

wrongAnswerFeedback.B: Logic can be distributed as needed.
wrongAnswerFeedback.C: APIs are still required for communication between services.
wrongAnswerFeedback.D: No architecture can guarantee zero downtime.
